7-Generation GTB

An initiative fostering social and ecological regeneration in the Greater Tkaronto Bioregion through a Bioregional Learning Center, portfolio of regenerative projects, and bioregional funding ecosyste

Bioregional Observatory/4 Bioregional Organizations/7-Generation GTB.md
Activities
33314415-10e1-81a1-aeb8-e12c4104edac, 33314415-10e1-811b-aaf6-e235ee839e71, 33314415-10e1-818b-ad2e-fd55264c84bc, 33314415-10e1-81aa-ac7c-fc89a3844272, 33314415-10e1-8183-a096-d8cc79dc3e43, 33314415-10e1-813e-a90e-c4e4d26d3ded
type
organization
location_lat
43.7
background_section
## Background The Greater Toronto Area (GTA) is Canada's most populous urban region, encompassing the city of Toronto and surrounding municipalities across the Golden Horseshoe. Situated along the northwestern shore of Lake Ontario, the GTA includes a mix of dense urban centers, suburban sprawl, agricultural lands, and remnants of the Carolinian forest zone—one of the most biodiverse ecosystems in the country. The name "Tkaronto" comes from the Mohawk word meaning "where there are trees standing in water," referring to the original Indigenous name for the area. This bioregion anchors the work of 7-Generation GTB, an initiative growing a metaphorical Tree of Life through social and ecological regeneration for ecopsychosocial wellbeing—people, community, and planet. ### Origins and Network The initiative began with a foundational question: "Where will we be in 50 years, if we continue our current development trajectory?" This inquiry, explored across various sectors over two years, shaped the organization's founding process and guided the formation of its first projects. The Legacy Project serves as the core team for 7-Generation GTB, coordinated by Brian Puppa. In the context of the GTB as part of Canada, the initiative weaves together holistic Indigenous knowledge systems with Western scientific thinking to find a "third way" forward. The network includes partnerships with Toronto and Region Conservation Authority (TRCA)—the largest conservation authority in the GTB and largest provider of outdoor education in Canada—Toronto Nature Stewards, York Regional Forest, Rouge National Urban Park, universities, libraries, regenerative farms, and community organizations.

description
An initiative fostering social and ecological regeneration in the Greater Tkaronto Bioregion through a Bioregional Learning Center, portfolio of regenerative projects, and bioregional funding ecosystem, weaving together Indigenous and Western knowledge systems.
id
greater-tkaronto
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy 7-Generation GTB works at once locally and in a **glocal** frame (place-based and globally connected), pairing social with ecological regeneration. It takes guidance from the Earth Charter while staying rooted in this bioregion. The GTB Bioregional Learning Center can operate as a 7-generation space that brings different ways of knowing together, puts life at the center of choices and action, and builds a shared story of place and bioregional identity that supports collective action across social and ecological work.
website
https://legacyproject.org/7gen/index.html
bioregional_context
The Greater Toronto Area is Canada's most populous urban region, encompassing Toronto and surrounding municipalities along Lake Ontario. The region includes dense urban centers, suburban sprawl, agricultural lands, and remnants of the Carolinian forest zone—one of the most biodiverse ecosystems in the country.
